description.en: Learn polymorphism and duck typing — how Python lets different objects respond to the same interface, regardless of their class.

story_variants.en:
## The Universal Remote

A **universal remote** has a single PLAY button. Press it on your TV and it plays a show. Press it on your music player and it plays a song. Same button, different behaviour.

This is **polymorphism**: one interface, many implementations.

### Two Faces of Polymorphism

| Type | How |
|------|-----|
| Subtype | child class overrides parent method |
| Duck typing | any object with the right method works |

### Duck Typing
> *"If it walks like a duck and quacks like a duck, it's a duck."*

Python doesn't care what class an object is — only whether it has the method being called.

```python
def make_sound(animal):
    print(animal.speak())  # works for Dog, Cat, any class with .speak()
```

> **Advantage:** Duck typing means you write flexible code that works with classes you haven't even written yet — as long as they follow the same interface.

### Abstract Base Classes
For enforcing an interface, use `abc.ABC` and `@abstractmethod` to require subclasses to implement certain methods.

instruction.en: Create classes 'Circle' and 'Rectangle', each with an 'area()' method. Write a function 'total_area(shapes)' that sums all areas.

hints.en: total_area loops over shapes and sums shape.area()

instruction.en: Demonstrate duck typing: write a function 'describe(obj)' that calls obj.describe() if it exists, else returns 'no description'.

hints.en: If True, return obj.describe(); else return 'no description'
Create a class Apple with describe() returning 'A red apple'
